title: Uphold the Discipline Periyar Spoke Of; Come Along Anna’s Path!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. The Tamil section is a source-checked reading copy derived from the OCR and does not alter the canonical source file. Visual review restored the opening `உடன்பிறப்பே,` and confirmed `33 பேர்`, `வேட்பாளர்களும், பொறுப்பாளர்களும் தந்த அறிக்கை`, and `நாடாளுமன்றத் தேர்தல்` in passages where OCR introduced numeral or Latin-script junk. Other repairs are limited to unambiguous broken word joins and glyph errors. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Even if a little smoke appears within the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, some Tamil Nadu newspapers and weeklies magnify it as though an enormous fire has broken out and many thousands have perished. At the same time, even when a great fire breaks out in the opposing camp, those same publications write as though it were nothing more than ordinary smoke.

One example is the way they reported the DMK’s defeat in the recently concluded parliamentary election. At the same time, they dream that the news of the Bengaluru disproportionate-assets case moving toward judgment can simply be concealed.

The 22-6-2014 issue of *Kumudam Reporter* wrote: “The principal demand made by many grassroots office-bearers and cadres is that the dominance of a few senior district secretaries who behave like petty chieftains must be ended. A senior party functionary said: ‘The AIADMK took swift action merely for losing three constituencies in the parliamentary election. DMK cadres now want action in that same style.’” Under the headline “Eighteen District Secretaries to Lose Their Posts?” it published the names of eighteen district secretaries. This is only one example.

They continued writing in this fashion. Under the heading “An Open Letter to Kalaignar,” the magazine *Siren* published a long article, purportedly written by a loving cadre, saying, “Leader, put down the pen and take up the whip!” The cover of the 28-5-2014 issue of *Junior Vikatan* carried a long article under the headline “Remove Every District Secretary.”
None of these articles was written out of concern for the movement. Their fond hope was: “Will nothing happen inside the DMK? If something does, can we not uproot the Dravidian movement itself?”

The DMK is a crop that will endure for a thousand years! Many formidable forces have tried to destroy it, only to meet defeat. The DMK has suffered electoral defeats many times, including the recent parliamentary result. But it has risen afterward with renewed vigour. Was there not an election in which Aasai Thambi alone won a single seat for the movement? Did the DMK alliance not go on to win thirty-eight seats in the following parliamentary election?

Electoral victory and defeat are like day and night. They will alternate. Has the BJP, now victorious at the Centre, never known defeat? Has the AIADMK never been defeated in Tamil Nadu? Has Jayalalithaa herself not lost an Assembly election? Did that party not forfeit its deposit in the Pennagaram by-election? Victory and defeat do not determine the lifespan of a movement.

Even so, after the parliamentary result, we heard that in some places members had not worked properly; district secretaries and senior functionaries had committed wrongs; and some had accepted money from the AIADMK and stayed away from election work. Under a decision of the movement’s High-Level Strategy Committee, we constituted a six-member committee.

After deliberating, that committee submitted a report. Acting upon it, we divided the existing thirty-four party districts into sixty-five and published full details of the Assembly constituencies that would fall within each.
Did the publications that had devoted page after page to demanding action praise this decision? No. Absolutely not.

The candidates who contested on behalf of the movement and the functionaries appointed by headquarters were asked to report on its election work. They submitted those reports. Headquarters appointed two people to compile and examine them, determine against whom complaints had been made, and recommend where action was warranted. For two or three days and nights, the two remained at headquarters, studying the nature of every complaint made by candidates and functionaries and deciding whom to recommend for action.

Even then, the movement did not reach an arbitrary verdict against those accused. They were not immediately removed from party membership. On the basis of those recommendations, they were temporarily relieved only of the offices they held and asked to answer the complaints within one week. Headquarters issued the announcement on 21-6-2014.
Afterward, all but one or two of those affected submitted explanations to headquarters and sent me copies. I looked through some of them.

Yesterday evening, a union secretary who had been asked to explain came to see me with about one hundred people from his constituency and gave me a copy. He wrote that he had studied only up to Standard V but that his son held a B.E. degree. He had therefore tried to secure a parliamentary-election candidacy for his son. The person eventually selected to contest from that constituency had resented him and, for that reason, filed a false complaint. He showed me photographic copies documenting his work for the movement and evidence of four cases filed against him by the ruling party because of his election work.
I asked him about the approximately one hundred people from his constituency who had come after the election to complain against him. He said the candidate had paid and brought them.

I told him: “You were asked for an explanation precisely so that all these details could be learned. The action based on the reports given by the candidates and functionaries is only temporary. The movement will carefully study both their complaints and the explanation you have now supplied, discover the truth, and only then make its final decision.” The union secretary and those who accompanied him returned reassured.

That is one example. In another district, two or three people asked for explanations came together, submitted their statements at headquarters and left copies at my home because they could not hand them to me personally. They asked that the Leader somehow be persuaded to protect them. Except for one or two, all those temporarily relieved have submitted explanations describing their past work for the movement, the hardships they endured, and the nature of the present accusations.

Those journalists eagerly proclaimed: “The movement has acted against thirty-three people. The movement is finished. One or two of the thirty-three have begun issuing severe statements against the party leadership and treasurer. The movement no longer exists.” They rushed to interview every person affected, hoping for a word or two against the leadership that could be inflated and given a sensational headline. They have met with a great disappointment.

If those temporarily affected truly believed the movement was finished after losing every parliamentary constituency and would never rise again, would they have come with so many people to explain themselves? They fall down and cry: “We did no wrong. The complaint is false. Allow us to continue working in the movement.” This is the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam! Is this what they imagine they can destroy?

The union secretary who came to explain and those with him wept. What benefit had they obtained from the movement? Were they yearning to become MLAs? Dreaming of becoming MPs? It was a defeated movement, yet they wished to remain, travelled many miles to explain themselves, fell at my feet and pleaded not to be expelled.

My precious Udanpirappugal, are they not the veins carrying the movement’s blood? The movement will never act against a cadre without understanding the truth and investigating fully. At the same time, if the complaints are true, and if one or two have done wrong in expectation of some reward, the movement will not refrain from acting. It will certainly not punish anyone by treating an empty accusation as truth.

Nor is this limited to those in the movement’s middle ranks. I know very well that some people at the top, including those regarded as senior pioneers, unwittingly engaged in wrongful conduct during this election. Some may wonder: “Will no action be taken against them? Were we alone at fault?” Do you imagine that what occurs to you does not come to my attention?

There are others who form their own groups in their districts, conduct meetings in those groups’ names, display their own photographs prominently, and exclude photographs of other office-bearers. These actions have not escaped my attention. Rather than regarding the AIADMK as the opposing party and enemy, they treat our own people as their closest enemies. When will they understand that such behaviour was one reason for our election defeat?

Parliamentary election victories and Assembly election results alone do not constitute the movement’s history. In a great movement like the DMK, we will not become frenzied merely because we win an election, nor will we collapse because we lose one.

I am not prepared to believe that everyone except those temporarily acted against did nothing wrong and worked excellently during the election. They may have escaped action for now because the candidates’ and functionaries’ reports made no specific complaint against them. But just as one who eats salt must drink water, those who did wrong must undergo punishment. The day may be delayed, that is all.

During the meeting of the High-Level Strategy Committee, one member said: “Leader, you will surely not act against anyone. Your heart is too tender, and you will never bring yourself to act.” I said nothing then.

Perarignar Anna once said that four must be made five and five must be made six, but six must never be reduced to five. It is true that I too keep this in mind. I am not in the position of certain parties where one person may remain if the leader wishes, but will be driven out if that leader does not.

Yet I am firm that when wrongdoing exceeds all bounds, action must be taken. Anna said what I have recalled. Periyar, speaking of Duty, Dignity and Discipline, said that Discipline was the most important of the three for a movement.
Discipline is therefore supremely important to our movement. Udanpirappē, the appeal of this letter is that every one of you must help preserve it.
With affection,
M.K.
